title: Learning objectives
text:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

Collect, organize, and clean dairy farm datasets derived from herd management systems.
Apply statistical and computational tools (e.g., Excel, R, Python, or similar software) to analyze dairy performance data.
Evaluate the strengths, weaknesses, and limitations of different data types used in dairy management.
Interpret and critically assess dairy datasets within biological, economic, and management contexts.
Create effective data visualizations, written reports, and presentations that communicate data-driven insights while adhering to ethical data use principles.
Synthesize herd-level data into a decision-support framework and collaboratively contribute to SWOT analyses with herd managers. ##

title: Project tips + resources
text:
Tips

Ask questions if any of the expectations are unclear.
Code: In your write up your code should be hidden so that your document is neat and easy to read. However your document should include all your code such that if I re-knit your qmd file I should be able to obtain the results you presented.

Exception: If you want to highlight something specific about a piece of code, you’re welcome to show that portion.

Merge conflicts will happen, issues will arise, and that’s fine! Commit and push often, and ask questions when stuck.
Make sure each team member is contributing, both in terms of quality and quantity of contribution (we will be reviewing commits from different team members).
All team members are expected to contribute equally to the completion of this assignment and group assessments will be given at its completion - anyone judged to not have sufficient contributed to the final product will have their grade penalized. While different teams members may have different backgrounds and abilities, it is the responsibility of every team member to understand how and why all code and approaches in the assignment works.
You can also chose to visualize your results after data processing in Tableau. Tableau is a powerfull and intuitive data visualisation tool with many options to make figures look nice.
On the website of the Bovi-Analytics lab there are also tons of tutorials to help get you started using the tools in the course. tutorials

title: Project tips + resources
text:
Formatting + communication tips

Suppress Code, Warnings, & Messages

Include the following code in a code chunk at the top of your .qmd file to suppress all code, warnings, and other messages.

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")


Headers

Use headers to clearly label each section.
Inspect the document outline to review your headers and sub-headers.



References

Include all references in a section called “References” at the end of the report.
This course does not have specific requirements for formatting citations and references.



Appendix

If you have additional work that does not fit or does not belong in the body of the report, you may put it at the end of the document in section called “Appendix”.
The items in the appendix should be properly labeled.
The appendix should only be for additional material. The reader should be able to fully understand your report without viewing content in the appendix.



Resize figures
Resize plots and figures, so you have more space for the narrative. Example:
#| fig-width: 6 #| fig-height: 4


Arranging plots
Arrange plots in a grid, instead of one after the other. This is especially useful when displaying plots for exploratory data analysis and to check assumptions. In Python Matplotlib has a function subplot() to do this. Look for documentation and examples how to make graphs look nice at matplotlib tutorials.


Plot titles and axis labels
Be sure all plot titles and axis labels are visible and easy to read.

Use informative titles, not variable names, for titles and axis labels.

❌ NO! The x-axis is hard to read because the names overlap.
sns.countplot(data=mpg, x="manufacturer")
plt.show()
✅ YES! Names are readable
sns.countplot(data=mpg, y="manufacturer")
plt.show()


Do a little more to make the plot look professional!

Informative title and axis labels
Flipped coordinates to make names readable
Arranged bars based on count
Capitalized manufacturer names
Optional: Added color - Use a coordinated color scheme throughout paper / presentation
Optional: Applied a theme - Use same theme throughout paper / presentation

counts = (
mpg["manufacturer"]
.str.title()
.value_counts()
.sort_values()
)

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
counts.plot(kind="barh", color="steelblue")
plt.xlabel("Count")
plt.ylabel("Manufacturer")
plt.title("Most Common Car Manufacturers")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Tables and model output

Use the kable function from the knitr package to neatly output all tables and model output. This will also ensure all model coefficients are displayed.

Use the digits argument to display only 3 or 4 significant digits.
Use the caption argument to add captions to your table.


import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ hp", data=mtcars).fit()
model.summary2().tables[1].round(3)


Guidelines for communicating results

Don’t use variable names in your narrative! Use descriptive terms, so the reader understands your narrative without relying on the data dictionary.

❌ There is a negative linear relationship between mpg and hp.
✅ There is a negative linear relationship between a car’s fuel economy (in miles per gallon) and its horsepower.

Know your audience: Your report should be written for a general audience who has an understanding of statistics at the level of STA 210.
Avoid subject matter jargon: Don’t assume the audience knows all of the specific terminology related to your subject area. If you must use jargon, include a brief definition the first time you introduce a term.
Tell the “so what”: Your report and presentation should be more than a list of interpretations and technical definitions. Focus on what the results mean, i.e. what you want the audience to know about your topic after reading your report or viewing your presentation.

❌ For every one unit increase in horsepower, we expect the miles per gallon to decrease by 0.068 units, on average.
✅ If the priority is to have good fuel economy, then one should choose a car with lower horsepower. Based on our model, the fuel economy is expected to decrease, on average, by 0.68 miles per gallon for every 10 additional horsepower.

Tell a story: All visualizations, tables, model output, and narrative should tell a cohesive story!
Use one voice: Though multiple people are writing the report, it should read as if it’s from a single author. At least one team member should read through the report before submission to ensure it reads like a cohesive document.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text: This course introduces students to the principles and applications of data analytics in modern dairy herd management. Students will learn how to collect, clean, analyze, and interpret farm- level data from the Cornell University Research Dairy. Through hands-on labs, students will apply statistical and computational tools to evaluate herd performance in key management areas, including nutrition, reproduction, animal health, and farm economics. By the end of the course, students will integrate these data into a digital management framework and engage in a managerial decision-making exercise, including a SWOT analysis with farm stakeholders. This course emphasizes data literacy, statistical reasoning, and ethical interpretation of agri- cultural data. It satisfies the CALS Data Literacy (Statistics) requirement by develop- ing student competencies in data manipulation, analysis, interpretation, and communication within the context of dairy science.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Lectures
The goal of the lectures is to have them as interactive as possible (which requires your attendance and participation). My role as instructor is to introduce you new tools and techniques, but it is up to you to take them and make use of them.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Labs
Labs will use real-world datasets from the Cornell University Research Centre. Students will work individually and in teams to: - Work with herd management software across the different management domains. - Clean and curate datasets (Excel, R, Python and Tableau). - Conduct statistical analyses on herd-level data. - Develop visualizations, reports and presentations for practical decision-making. - Interact with students and herd managers to interpret results in a farm management context.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Individual assessment
It is very important to read this part as your individual assessments will be influencing your individual gradings.
At the end of the course you will be evaluated on the % of weekly assignments you have coded correctly in the Dodona framework (which you will learn during the course). That percentage will be multiplied with the overal group project grade, resulting in your final individual grade.
For example:

The group project was finally graded at 95%
Student A successfully finished 75% of the coding assignments within each deadline. As a result student A gets 95% x 75% = 71% as the final individual grade.
Student B successfully finished 100% of the coding assignments within each deadline. As a result student B gets 95% x 100% as the final individual grade.

Finishing each of the assignments within each deadline is not that difficult. You “just” need to devote the time to it. I have used this method a lot during my courses. It is not to annoy students, but to make sure the entire group keeps progressing on the programming skills during the project. You will thank me for that at a certain moment. For full transparency, I learned to code Python using a similar approach. Once I understood why this was done, I started appreciating this method as one of the most inspiring learning methods I have ever seen.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Inclusive community
I grew up in a family in which values as diversity, equity and inclusion were at the core of our everyday life. My parents were both involved taking care of people struggling with equity and inclusion, and as a result these values are deeply embedded in my character.
I aim to ensure that students from all diverse backgrounds and perspectives are well-served by this course. I strive to address students’ learning needs both in and out of class, and to view the diversity that students bring as a resource, strength, and benefit. My goal is to present materials and activities that respect diversity and align with Cornell University’s core values. Sometimes it might fade during busy times, don’t be afraid to recall someone, we’re all humans after all. “Your suggestions are truely encouraged and appreciated”. Please let me know how I can improve the course’s effectiveness for you personally, or for other students or student groups.
Additionally, I aim to foster a learning environment that embraces a diversity of thoughts, perspectives, and experiences, and respects your identities. If your experiences outside of class are affecting your performance, please feel free to talk with me. Alternatively, your academic dean is a great resource if you prefer to speak with someone outside the course.

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Academic Integrity
Absolute integrity is expected of every Cornell student in all academic undertakings. Integrity entails a firm adherence to a set of values, and the values most essential to an academic community are grounded on the concept of honesty with respect to the intellectual efforts of oneself and others, and free and open inquiry and discussion in the classroom. Academic integrity is expected not only in formal coursework situations, but in all University relationships and interactions connected to the educational process, including the use of University resources. While both students and faculty of Cornell assume the responsibility of maintaining and furthering these values, this document is concerned specifically with the conduct of students.
A Cornell student’s submission of work for academic credit indicates that the work is the student’s own. All outside assistance should be acknowledged, and the student’s academic position truthfully reported at all times. In addition, Cornell students have a right to expect academic integrity from each of their peers.
This a guideline for students offered through the Office of the Dean of Faculty

title: Digital Dairy Management and Data Analytics
text:
Responsible Use of Generative Tools
In this class, we recognize the potential of generative tools to enhance learning and creativity. However, it is crucial to use these tools responsibly and ethically. Here are some guidelines to ensure their proper use:

Academic Integrity: Always attribute the work generated by these tools appropriately. Do not present AI-generated content as your own original work without proper citation.
Critical Thinking: Use generative tools as a supplement to your own thinking and analysis. Evaluate the outputs critically and ensure they align with the academic standards and objectives of our course.
Ethical Considerations: Be mindful of the ethical implications of using generative tools. Avoid generating content that could be harmful, misleading, or inappropriate.
Privacy and Security: Respect privacy and confidentiality when using generative tools. Do not input sensitive or personal information into these tools.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Big idea
Every number you see on a farm is an estimate.

It has sampling uncertainty (finite cows, finite days)
It has measurement uncertainty (sensor error, missingness)
It has model uncertainty (assumptions, misspecification)

Today we learn how to reason, quantify, and communicate that uncertainty.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Learning goals (part 1)

Translate a dairy question into (data type → model → test)
Run and interpret:

a hypothesis test for a normal outcome (mean difference)
a hypothesis test for a binomial outcome (proportion difference)

Explain the true value of a p-value (what it is / is not)

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Learning goals (part 2)

Distinguish statistically different vs biologically different
Distinguish association vs prediction, and interpret Se/Sp/PPV/NPV/Accuracy


Key teaching emphasis: Students often confuse “significant” with “important” and “correlation” with “usable prediction”.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Where does uncertainty come from?
A. Sampling (finite cows, finite events)
Example: pregnancy rate on 25 AIs is noisier than on 250 AIs
B. Measurement (sensors, humans, lab assays)
Example: activity sensor misses mild estrus; SCC lab has CV

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Where does uncertainty come from?
C. Model (assumptions)
Example: t-test assumes approximate normality / equal variances
D. Decision context (costs and consequences)
Example: a mastitis alert that fires too often wastes time

Bring this back later: statistical test choice is about data-generating process.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
“Uncertainty” is not the enemy
In dairy science we often use data to:

Understand (association, mechanism)
Decide (intervention, management)
Predict (risk scores, alerts)

Uncertainty can be:

Quantified (SE, CI, posterior)
Reduced (better design, more cows, better sensors)
Managed (thresholds, decision analysis)

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
What a p-value is
A p-value is:

The probability (under the null hypothesis) of observing a result as extreme or more extreme than what you observed.

So it is a statement about data under a model, not about “truth”.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
What a p-value is not
A p-value is NOT:

The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability your result will replicate
A measure of effect size or biological relevance
A “proof” of causality


Students often interpret p=0.03 as “97% chance the treatment works”. That is wrong.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Why reporting exact p-values matters
When reporting results:

Prefer exact p-values (e.g., P = 0.003; P = 0.71) rather than only “P < 0.05”.
Prefer effect sizes + confidence intervals around effects.

This matches good reporting guidance in dairy research.
Example reporting guidance: quantify p-values to <0.001 and prefer 95% confidence intervals around effect estimates. (Lean et al., 2016)[doi:10.3168/jds.2015-9445]

This point is explicitly recommended in the attached JDS invited review.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Data science × statistics: the handshake
Statistics helps you:

Choose a model aligned with the data-generating process
Quantify uncertainty (SE/CI)
Avoid false discoveries / overconfidence

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Data science × statistics: the handshake
Data science helps you:

Build reproducible pipelines
Validate models out-of-sample
Scale to large, messy farm data

Best practice: combine both.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 1 (Normal): milk yield change vs a target
text:
Example 1 (Normal): milk yield change vs a target
Question: A farm says the new ration should increase 30-d milk yield by +4 lbs/day. We sample 24 cows and compute their change (after–before).

Outcome: continuous (lbs/day)
Design: paired (same cows)
Model: approx normal differences
Test: paired t-test

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Code (R)

n <- 24
true_effect <- 2.4     # true mean change (lbs/d)
sd_change <- 3.0

change <- rnorm(n, mean=true_effect, sd=sd_change)

# H0: mean change = 4 lbs/d (the claim) HA: mean change != 4
res <- t.test(change, mu = 4)
res


    One Sample t-test

data:  change
t = -3.6805, df = 23, p-value = 0.001239
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 4
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.3950024 2.9893134
sample estimates:
mean of x 
 1.692158 



This is a deliberately realistic scenario: lots of biological noise. Ask: even if p>0.05, what is the CI around the effect?

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Interpreting the result: emphasize effect size
text:
Interpreting the result: emphasize effect size
The test checks whether the mean of the variable change is equal to 4.
Hypotheses:

H0: The mean = 4
H1: The mean ≠ 4

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
What does the t-value mean?
t = -3.7808

The negative sign means the sample mean is below 4
The larger the absolute value of t, the stronger the evidence against H0

This suggests the mean is far from 4.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Degrees of Freedom
df = 23
This means:

Number of observations = 24
(because df = n − 1)

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
The P-value
P-value = 0.0009681
What this means:

The probability of getting this result if the true mean were 4 is very small
Since 0.0009681< 0.05, the result is statistically significant

Conclusion:
We reject the null hypothesis.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Confidence Interval
95% Confidence Interval:
1.077941 → 3.144723
Important:

The value 4 is not inside the interval
This confirms that the mean is significantly different from 4

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Sample Mean
Mean of change = 2.111332
Interpretation:

The average value is about 2.11
This is clearly lower than 4

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Final Conclusion
The data show that:

The mean of change is significantly different from 4
The true mean is most likely between 1.08 and 3.14
The sample mean is 2.11

Final conclusion:

The average change is significantly lower than 4.
Is it biologically meaningful for the farm?

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 2 (Binomial): conception rate vs a baseline
text:
Example 2 (Binomial): conception rate vs a baseline
Question: In a timed-AI protocol, baseline P/AI = 0.35. After a management tweak we observe x = 18 pregnancies out of n = 40 AIs.

Outcome: pregnancy (0/1)
Model: binomial
Test: binomial test (one-sample proportion)

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 2 (Binomial): conception rate vs a baseline
text:
Example 2 (Binomial): conception rate vs a baseline

x <- 18
n <- 40
p0 <- 0.35

#Binomial test
binom.test(x, n, p = p0, alternative = "two.sided")


    Exact binomial test

data:  x and n
number of successes = 18, number of trials = 40, p-value = 0.1883
alternative hypothesis: true probability of success is not equal to 0.35
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.2925884 0.6150932
sample estimates:
probability of success 
                  0.45 



Highlight: with n=40, uncertainty is still big. Ask students to inspect CI for the proportion.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments
text:
Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments
Question: Treatment A vs B; outcome is pregnant yes/no.

If expected counts are large → chi-square / two-proportion test
If expected counts are small → Fisher exact

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments
text:
Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments

# Example counts
# A: 55 preg / 145 not
# B: 70 preg / 130 not

tab <- matrix(c(55,145,70,130), nrow=2, byrow=TRUE)
colnames(tab) <- c("Preg", "Not")
rownames(tab) <- c("A", "B")
tab

  Preg Not
A   55 145
B   70 130

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments
text:
Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments

#Chi-square
prop.test(tab)


    2-sample test for equality of proportions with continuity correction

data:  tab
X-squared = 2.2807, df = 1, p-value = 0.131
alternative hypothesis: two.sided
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.17054896  0.02054896
sample estimates:
prop 1 prop 2 
 0.275  0.350

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments
text:
Example 3 (Two groups): pregnancy probability in 2 treatments

#Fisher exact
fisher.test(tab)


    Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data

data:  tab
p-value = 0.1308
alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.4497776 1.1014281
sample estimates:
odds ratio 
 0.7050632 



Teach students: prop.test uses approximate methods; fisher is exact for small counts.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Quick map: outcome → typical distribution

Milk yield (daily): roughly normal after removing extremes; often needs mixed models
SCC: often log-normal (or gamma) after log transform
Clinical mastitis events per lactation: Poisson-like, but often overdispersed → negative binomial
Time to pregnancy: survival/time-to-event
Proportions (e.g., % infected): beta distribution on (0,1)


You can show raw SCC vs log(SCC) histograms to motivate transformations.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Example: SCC looks log-normal (why we log-transform)
text:
Example: SCC looks log-normal (why we log-transform)


Connect this to model assumptions: many tests/regressions behave better after transformation.

text: Visual: same mean, different uncertainty

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Poisson vs negative binomial
Question: Count of clinical mastitis cases per cow-year.

Poisson assumes mean = variance
Dairy health data often have variance > mean (overdispersion) → negative binomial

text:
Poisson vs negative binomial

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Time-to-pregnancy is survival data


Use this slide to reinforce: for time-to-event outcomes, survival analysis is the right tool and avoids bias from excluding cows that never conceive.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Why “statistically significant” can be misleading
You can get P < 0.05 when:

The effect is tiny but n is huge
The effect is real but irrelevant for welfare, economics, or practicality

You can also get P > 0.05 when:

The effect might be meaningful but n is small
There is high biological variability

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Why “statistically significant” can be misleading
So always report:

Effect size
Confidence interval
Context-specific threshold (“what difference matters?”)

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Practical tool: define a “biological minimum important difference”
text:
Practical tool: define a “biological minimum important difference”
Examples (illustrative):

Milk yield: +2 lbs/d might matter economically on some farms
Conception rate: +3 to +5 percentage points might matter if sustained
Disease incidence: -2% incidence might matter if treatment cost is high


Let students propose what “biologically meaningful” means for one KPI.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Sample size: how many cows do we need?
text:
Sample size: how many cows do we need?
Effect size you care about drives sample size.
From the invited review on reproductive intervention studies:

To detect an increase in pregnancy probability per insemination from 35% to 40% with power = 0.8 and α = 0.05, requires 1,471 cows per group.
Additional cows may be required when there is clustering (e.g., cows within herds). (Lean et al., 2016)[doi:10.3168/jds.2015-9445]

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Association answers “is X related to Y?”
text:
Association answers “is X related to Y?”

Goal: inference, explanation, mechanism
Output: coefficients, CI, p-values
Example: higher rumination is associated with lower disease odds

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: Prediction answers “can we forecast Y for a new cow?”
text:
Prediction answers “can we forecast Y for a new cow?”

Goal: accurate out-of-sample performance
Output: sensitivity, specificity, PPV/NPV, ROC-AUC, calibration
Example: build an early-lactation alert for ketosis

A model can have association without useful prediction.

Key message: prediction needs validation on new data and pays attention to prevalence and decision thresholds.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: A quick demonstration: strong association, weak prediction
text:
A quick demonstration: strong association, weak prediction
If an outcome is rare, even a decent model can have low PPV.

Prevalence: 5% disease
Sensitivity: 80%
Specificity: 90%

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
section: A quick demonstration: strong association, weak prediction
text:
A quick demonstration: strong association, weak prediction
Let’s compute PPV/NPV.

prev <- 0.05
se <- 0.80
sp <- 0.90

ppv <- (se*prev) / (se*prev + (1-sp)*(1-prev))
npv <- (sp*(1-prev)) / ((1-se)*prev + sp*(1-prev))

ppv; npv

[1] 0.2962963


[1] 0.9884393

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Confusion matrix (definitions)
Let:

TP = true positives
FP = false positives
TN = true negatives
FN = false negatives

Then:

Sensitivity (Se) = TP / (TP + FN)
Specificity (Sp) = TN / (TN + FP)
Accuracy = (TP + TN) / total
PPV = TP / (TP + FP)
NPV = TN / (TN + FN)

PPV/NPV depend strongly on prevalence.

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Worked dairy example: mastitis alert
Assume 1,000 cows monitored in a period:

True mastitis prevalence = 8% → 80 true cases
Algorithm: Se = 75%, Sp = 92%

Compute counts:


 TP  FP  TN  FN 
 60  74 846  20 


 accuracy       PPV       NPV 
0.9060000 0.4477612 0.9769053 



If the algorithm flags a cow as having mastitis, there’s about a 45% chance it actually has mastitis.
So negatives are much more reliable than positives here.


Discuss: Is PPV high enough to justify an alert? What is the cost of FP vs FN on this farm?

title: Lecture – Working with uncertainty in dairy science
text:
Why regression shows up everywhere
A linear regression is:

A model for continuous outcomes
With predictors (continuous or categorical)

ANOVA is a special case:

Same linear model
Predictors can be categorical group indicators

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this lecture, students will be able to:

Explain what tables, keys, and relationships are using dairy data
Distinguish between different types of joins
Decide which join to use for cow and milk production data
Implement joins and relationships in Excel and Tableau
Diagnose common join-related data problems in animal records


~3 minutes. Set expectations and connect to previous data topics in dairy science.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
section: Why this matters in dairy data
text:
Why this matters in dairy data
Dairy data is rarely stored in one table:

Cow information
Daily milk production records
Health or reproduction events

To answer questions, we must combine tables correctly.

Use examples like average milk yield per cow or missing production days.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
section: From flat files to relational thinking
text:
From flat files to relational thinking

One big spreadsheet with everything seems easy… at first
Repeating cow information every day causes:

Errors
Inconsistencies
Very large files


Relational data solves this by splitting data into tables.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
What is a table?
A table is:

Rows = observations (records)
Columns = variables (fields)

In our example:

Cows
MilkProduction

Each table describes one thing.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
The idea of a key
A key is a column that identifies rows.

Primary key: uniquely identifies a row

CowID

Foreign key: refers to a key in another table

CowID inside MilkProduction



Emphasize that CowID must be unique and stable over time.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Relationships vs joins (important!)
Two ways to combine cow and production tables:

Relationships (logical connection)
Joins (physical merge)

Tableau makes this distinction explicit. Excel mostly works with joins.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Relationships (conceptual)
A relationship says:

“These tables are related by CowID.”


Tables stay separate
Combination happens when needed
Less risk of duplicating cow information


This is Tableau’s default approach.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Joins (physical)
A join:

Combines rows into a new table
Based on matching CowID values
Choice of join affects which records remain

This is where many data mistakes occur.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Visual intuition for joins
Think in terms of sets of cows:

Cows table
Milk production table
Overlap based on CowID

We now look at the four main joins.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text: Type of joins

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Inner join
Keeps only matching cows

CowID must exist in both tables
Drops production records without cow info

Use when: - You only want valid cows with production data

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Inner join example
Cows ⨝ MilkProduction

Cow 101 → kept
Cow 102 → kept
Cow 103 → dropped (no production)
Cow 104 → dropped (no cow record)

Result: only cows with valid production records.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Left join
Keeps all cows

Production data added when available
Missing production becomes NULL

Use when: - The cow list is your main population

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Left join example
Cows ← MilkProduction

All cows kept (101–103)
Cow 103 → Milk_kg = NULL

Very common for herd summaries.

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Right join
Keeps all production records

Even if CowID is missing in cow table

Rarely ideal, but useful for: - Detecting data quality issues

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
text:
Full outer join
Keeps all cows and all production records

Highlights mismatches clearly
Unmatched rows filled with NULLs

Use when: - Auditing or cleaning data

title: Using Relationships and Joins Between Tables
section: Common join pitfalls in dairy data
text:
Common join pitfalls in dairy data

Duplicate CowID values
Missing cows or production days
Text vs numeric CowID
Reused IDs across farms

Always check row counts after joining.

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Learning goals

For each KPI: bias, momentum, variance, lag
Calculate standard error for a rate and interpret it
Explain monitoring differences: small vs large herds
Build CUSUM/EWMA monitoring for small-n situations

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Repro performance “core”: 21-d pregnancy rate
On large farms, reproductive performance is often tracked with 21-day pregnancy rate, driven by:

AI service rate (submission)
P/AI (conception rate)

Your analytics should always decompose PR into IR × CR.

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Days in milk (DIM) to first insemination
text:
Days in milk (DIM) to first insemination

Synonyms: calving-to-first-service interval
Definition: DIM at first service.
Bias - VWP policy differences; health holds - Missing early heats/services shifts later
Momentum - Strong pipeline; slow changes
Variance - Moderate; clustered by calving season/pen
Lag - Responds after new calving cohorts pass VWP

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
DIM to first heat

Synonyms: days to first observed estrus postpartum
Bias - Under-detection (short estrus duration at high production) 1 - “Observed” ≠ “occurred”
Momentum - Affected by transition health & energy balance
Variance - High without sensors
Lag - First weeks postpartum

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Number of inseminations per pregnancy

Synonyms: services per conception, S/C
Definition: total services leading up to conception.
Bias - Censoring: open cows not yet pregnant - Mis-linked pregnancy to service
Momentum - Improves slowly; repeat breeders dominate
Variance - High; long tail distribution
Lag - Requires pregnancy outcome

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Number of inseminations per inseminated cow (insemination number)
text:
Number of inseminations per inseminated cow (insemination number)

Synonyms: services per bred cow
Definition: average number of services among cows with ≥1 service (in window).
Bias - Window truncation (late services fall outside) - Open cows inflate if tracked cross-sectionally
Momentum - Moderate
Variance - High at small n
Lag - Low to moderate

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Number of inseminations per non-pregnant cow
text:
Number of inseminations per non-pregnant cow

Synonyms: services per open cow
Definition: among currently open cows, average services received.
Bias - Survivorship/culling (high-service cows often leave) - Pregnancy status misclassification
Momentum - High inertia; management often reacts late
Variance - High; heavily skewed
Lag - Low (operational)

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Animals > 3 inseminations

Synonyms: repeat breeders, >3 services
Definition: proportion or count of cows with services ≥4 and not confirmed pregnant.
Bias - Depends on pregnancy diagnosis timing - Culling removes worst cases
Momentum - Sticky (accumulates)
Variance - Moderate
Lag - Builds over months

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Heat detection rate

Synonyms: estrus detection rate (EDR), detection efficiency
Definition (common): fraction of eligible cows in heat that are detected.
Practical proxy: “submission rate” (see service rate) when direct heat is unobservable.
Bias - True heats are unobserved; sensors change sensitivity - High milk production shortens estrus duration → apparent EDR drops 1
Momentum - Can change quickly with labor/sensors
Variance - High day-to-day, lower over weeks
Lag - Low

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Pre-Service Heat Detection Efficiency

Synonyms: pre-breeding detection efficiency
Definition: detection/submission performance before first service.
Bias - VWP, pen moves, health holds
Momentum - Moderate
Variance - Moderate
Lag - Short

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Post-Service Heat Detection Efficiency

Synonyms: return-to-estrus detection efficiency
Definition: how well returns to heat are detected after an AI.
Bias - Pregnancy loss delays return-to-estrus distribution 1
Momentum - Improves with monitoring/sensors
Variance - High with small n
Lag - Medium (depends on cycle length + diagnosis schedule)

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Total number of pregnancies

Definition: count of confirmed pregnancies (cross-sectional or within time window).
Bias - Recheck duplication or missing updates after loss
Momentum - Inventory-like
Variance - n-dependent
Lag - Preg-check schedule driven

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Total number of calvings

Definition: count of calving events.
Bias - Stillbirth/abortion recording differences
Momentum - Very slow; year-long pipeline
Variance - Low for large herds; high for small
Lag - Very long

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Days open

Synonyms: calving-to-conception interval
Definition: days from calving to conception (or to confirmed pregnancy date with correction).
Bias - If you use pregnancy confirmation date instead of conception date → overestimates - Censoring: cows not yet pregnant
Momentum - High inertia
Variance - Moderate
Lag - Medium-long

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Interval First Service – Conception

Synonyms: first-service-to-conception interval
Definition: days between first service and service that resulted in pregnancy.
Bias - Censoring; pregnancy loss misclassification
Momentum - Moderate
Variance - Skewed
Lag - Medium

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Calving interval (historic, expected, predicted, adjusted)

Synonyms: inter-calving interval (ICI)

Historic: actual previous calving-to-calving
Expected/Predicted: using current pregnancy due date
Adjusted: corrected for censored/open cows or policy (document your method)

Bias - Open cows missing; induced calving; pregnancy loss
Momentum - Very high inertia
Variance - Low in large herds
Lag - Very long

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Percentage pregnant animals

Synonyms: pregnancy prevalence
Definition: pregnant / (pregnant + eligible open) at a snapshot.
Bias - Eligibility definition; missing pregnancy loss updates
Momentum - Inventory inertia
Variance - Lower in large herds
Lag - Medium

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
% pregnant at 100 DIM

Synonyms: pregnancy by 100 DIM
Definition: among cows reaching 100 DIM, fraction pregnant by that point (cohort-friendly).
Bias - Culling before 100 DIM (survivorship bias) - Missing preg checks
Momentum - Medium-high
Variance - Moderate
Lag - Medium

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
% non-pregnant at 200 DIM

Synonyms: open rate at 200 DIM
Bias - Culling before 200 DIM inflates “good” performance
Momentum - High
Variance - Moderate
Lag - Long

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Insemination rate or risk

Synonyms: service rate, submission rate (SR)
Definition: eligible cows inseminated in a time window / eligible cows.
LDHM Book context: AI service rate is a key determinant of 21-d pregnancy rate.
Bias - Eligibility definition (VWP, health holds) - Missing services
Momentum - Can change quickly
Variance - High in small herds
Lag - Low

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Conception rate or risk

Synonyms: P/AI, pregnancies per AI
Definition: pregnancies from AIs / total AIs (with consistent pregnancy check timing).
LDHM Book context: P/AI is used as fertility measure for inseminated cows.
Bias - Pregnancy loss after check - Non-independent observations (same cow multiple AIs) - Tech/bull confounding
Momentum - Medium
Variance - High at small n
Lag - At least until pregnancy diagnosis

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Pregnancy rate or risk

Synonyms: 21-d pregnancy rate (when windowed), PR
Definition: eligible cows that become pregnant in window / eligible cows.
Book context: 21-d pregnancy rate is widely used as the primary herd metric on large farms. 1
Bias - Window definition + eligibility drift - Double-count pregnancies if not careful with event logic
Momentum - Medium
Variance - Can be very high in small herds
Lag - Medium

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Non-return % 56

Synonyms: NR56, 56-day nonreturn rate
Definition: proportion of inseminated cows that do not return to estrus within 56 days (often proxy for pregnancy).
Bias - Silent heats, missed heats, long cycles, pregnancy loss - Strongly affected by heat detection quality
Momentum - Medium
Variance - Moderate-high
Lag - Fixed by definition (56 days)

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Days lost due to suboptimal Heat Detection (post insemination)
text:
Days lost due to suboptimal Heat Detection (post insemination)
Idea: lost time between return to estrus and next service because heat wasn’t detected promptly.
Bias - Requires accurate return-to-estrus inference (hard if heats aren’t logged) - Pregnancy loss complicates return timing 1
Momentum - Can change quickly with detection tools
Variance - High
Lag - Medium

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Days lost due to suboptimal Conception Rate
text:
Days lost due to suboptimal Conception Rate
Idea: extra open days caused by failed conceptions (repeat AIs) versus a target CR.
Bias - Requires a counterfactual target; sensitive to censoring
Momentum - Medium
Variance - Depends on n
Lag - Medium-long

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
The basic binomial SE
For a proportion (rate) = x/n: [ SE() = ]
Interpretation: - The smaller n, the larger the SE (noisy rates) - For fixed n, SE is largest near p = 0.5

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
How to decrease SE (three levers)

Increase n

more cows in denominator (bigger herd)
longer time window (aggregate weeks)

Reduce clustering

stratify sensibly (don’t slice too thin by pen/tech/bull)

Use smoothing / sequential methods

EWMA, CUSUM (next section)


Rule of thumb: - To halve SE, you need ~4× the sample size.

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Interpreting SE in practice
Example logic (no need to memorize):

If CR = 35% on n = 20 AI
SE ≈ sqrt(0.35*0.65/20) ≈ 0.106 (~10.6 percentage points)

That means week-to-week swings are expected even if “true” CR is stable.

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Large herds

Weekly (or even daily) rates can be stable enough for decisions
You can stratify (parity, pen, tech) without destroying n
Control charts detect small shifts earlier

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Small herds

Weekly rates are dominated by random variation
“Pregnancy rate dropped from 30% to 10%” may be just n=10 vs n=10
You must:

aggregate longer (e.g., 6–12 weeks)
use smoothing/seq methods (EWMA/CUSUM)
focus on counts + timelines (who is overdue) alongside rates

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
section: Why IR/CR/PR can mislead at small n
text:
Why IR/CR/PR can mislead at small n

PR = IR × CR: both are noisy → PR is very noisy
A single pregnancy (or loss) can swing the rate dramatically
Therefore, use:

confidence intervals / SE
rolling windows
sequential charts

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average)
Purpose: smooth noisy weekly rates while staying responsive.
Update: [ EWMA_t = x_t + (1-)EWMA_{t-1} ] - x_t = this week’s rate (or 0/1 outcome stream) - small (0.1–0.3) = more smoothing
Interpretation: - EWMA shows momentum (trend) better than raw rates

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)
Purpose: detect sustained small shifts.
Binary-outcome version idea: - Compare observed outcomes to target p0 - Accumulate evidence: - add (x_t - p0) each time step - alarm when cum-sum crosses threshold
Interpretation: - CUSUM is great when n is small but you monitor continuously.

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Practical setup suggestion (small herd)

Track CR with:

rolling 8-week CR
EWMA of weekly CR (= 0.2)

Track IR similarly
Keep an operational list:

overdue to first service
3 services open
open >200 DIM

title: Lecture - Cow metrics
text:
Take-home

Always decompose PR into submission (IR) and success (CR)
Rates need uncertainty (SE/CI), especially in small herds
EWMA/CUSUM convert noisy signals into actionable monitoring

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Learning goals

Refresh core reproductive physiology (enough to interpret data)
Translate biology into questions your database must answer
Identify common failure modes (estrus, ovulation, luteolysis, anovulation)
Connect management levers to measurable outcomes


This deck is intentionally “physiology-light but analytics-heavy”. We focus on the minimum biology needed to interpret KPIs correctly.

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
section: Why repro is a data problem
text:
Why repro is a data problem

Reproductive efficiency drives:

Milk production dynamics (lactation length, calving pattern)
Replacement rate, culling risk, heifer inventory, genetic progress
Labor and treatment costs

The industry’s challenge: fertility declined then rebounded—requiring integration of physiology, management, nutrition, genetics, economics, and records.

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Conceptual model: “open → pregnant”
How fast the program turns eligible non-pregnant cows into pregnant cows.
Key workflow:

Calve → VWP (voluntary waiting period)
Detect heat or timed AI (TAI) program
Inseminate (service)

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Conceptual model: “open → pregnant”

Conceive (P/AI)
Maintain pregnancy (loss matters)
Diagnose (preg check)
Resync or rebreed non-pregnant cows

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Minimal physiology you must know
Estrous cycle in cattle:

~3 weeks, but longer in lactating cows vs heifers (≈23 d vs ≈21 d).
Ovarian structures:

Follicles (dominant follicle → ovulation)
Corpus luteum (CL → progesterone)
Luteolysis timing differs (heifers vs lactating cows).

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Minimal physiology you must know
Six key hormones and what they mean for data interpretation:

Progesterone (P4): from CL; maintains pregnancy; blocks estrus / GnRH surge
Estradiol (E2): from follicle; standing heat; triggers GnRH surge
GnRH: hypothalamus; triggers LH surge

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Minimal physiology you must know

LH: surge causes ovulation; pulses drive dominant follicle growth
FSH: initiates follicular wave
PGF2α: uterus; regresses CL (luteolysis)

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
section: What high milk production changes (and why metrics mislead)
text:
What high milk production changes (and why metrics mislead)
High milk production is associated with:

Larger ovulatory follicles but lower circulating E2
Larger luteal tissue volume but lower circulating P4
Shorter estrus duration at higher production
Higher risk of double ovulation (twinning risk)

Analytics consequence: “heat detection looks worse” partly because estrus expression is shorter, not only because labor/observation is worse.

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Estrus: behavior vs biology

Standing heat is driven by E2 and ends when E2 drops below threshold (often before ovulation).
AI timing should relate to onset of estrus (biologically tied to GnRH/LH surge), not just “was she seen?”
Therefore: “no observed heat” ≠ “no ovulation”

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Anovulation: big hidden driver

Anovulation commonly defined as failure to ovulate around ~60–80 DIM
Multi-study summaries show ~20–25% anovular cows around 60–70 DIM (large between-farm variation).
Two phenotypes (useful for interpreting progesterone/ultrasound data):

“Small-follicle” anovular (insufficient LH pulses → follicles don’t reach ovulatory size)
“Large-follicle/cystic” anovular (high E2 fails to induce GnRH/LH surge) 1

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Management lever: timed AI programs
Why TAI exists:

Detection-of-estrus-only programs often delay first service and reduce service rate.
Ovsynch (GnRH → PGF2α → GnRH → TAI) coordinates ovulation and CL regression, enabling timed insemination without estrus detection.
Presynchronization (PGF2α and/or GnRH combinations) aims to start protocols at an optimal cycle stage and address anovulation.

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
“What questions must the data answer?”
For any herd (and each pen/group):

Eligibility / exposure

Who is eligible to be bred this week (DIM, VWP, health holds)?

Submission

Who was submitted to insemination (observed heat or protocol)?

Service quality

Was the insemination recorded correctly (date/time, technician, semen, bull)?

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
“What questions must the data answer?”

Fertility outcome

Who conceived (preg check), who didn’t, who lost?

Timeliness

How long did it take from calving → first service → conception?

Bottleneck diagnosis

Is low pregnancy rate due to service rate or P/AI?

title: Lecture – Physiology basics → what data must answer
text:
Take-home summary

Physiology gives you mechanisms; metrics give you signals
High production alters hormone dynamics → affects estrus expression and twinning risk
Anovulation is common around breeding start and requires protocol-aware interpretation
Your monitoring framework must separate:

Exposure (service/submission) from success (conception/pregnancy)

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
What are the FAIR principles
FAIR data is data which meets the FAIR principles of findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability (FAIR).[1][2] The acronym and principles were defined in a March 2016 paper in the journal Scientific Data by a consortium of scientists and organizations.[1]

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Get to know the principles (1)

Get familiar with FAIR by looking at this video.
Read the intial publication on The FAIR Guiding Principles for scientific data management and stewardschip.
Explore the vision of Cornell University on FAIR principles and their cheatsheets.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Get to know the principles (2)

Check for reproducible advise in the tutorials at the bovi-analytics website.
Discuss with the entire team and Dr. Miel Hostens (contact him on his desk) on how this will reflect on your team project.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Findable
The first step in (re)using data is to find them. Metadata and data should be easy to find for both humans and computers. Machine-readable metadata are essential for automatic discovery of datasets and services, so this is an essential component of the FAIRification process.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Findable

F1. (Meta)data are assigned a globally unique and persistent identifier
F2. Data are described with rich metadata (defined by R1 below)
F3. Metadata clearly and explicitly include the identifier of the data they describe
F4. (Meta)data are registered or indexed in a searchable resource

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Accessible
Once the user finds the required data, they need to know how they can be accessed, possibly including authentication and authorisation.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Accessible

A1. (Meta)data are retrievable by their identifier using a standardised communications protocol
A1.1 The protocol is open, free, and universally implementable
A1.2 The protocol allows for an authentication and authorisation procedure, where necessary
A2. Metadata are accessible, even when the data are no longer available

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Interoperable
The data usually need to be integrated with other data. In addition, the data need to interoperate with applications or workflows for analysis, storage, and processing.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Interoperable

I1. (Meta)data use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation.
I2. (Meta)data use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles
I3. (Meta)data include qualified references to other (meta)data

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Reusable
The ultimate goal of FAIR is to optimise the reuse of data. To achieve this, metadata and data should be well-described so that they can be replicated and/or combined in different settings.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Reusable

R1. (Meta)data are richly described with a plurality of accurate and relevant attributes
R1.1. (Meta)data are released with a clear and accessible data usage license
R1.2. (Meta)data are associated with detailed provenance
R1.3. (Meta)data meet domain-relevant community standards

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Why do we need this

Dairy data science is multimodal, longitudinal, and collaborative
Naming is not cosmetic — it is infrastructure
Most downstream issues are caused by:

Ambiguity
Inconsistency
Silent semantic drift

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
40 Minutes, 4 Takeaways

Naming conventions are scientific decisions
Bad names silently break analyis and models
Conventions must scale across projects & people
Governance matters more than documentation

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
The Dairy Data Reality

Multiple farms
Multiple vendors
Multiple countries
Multiple disciplines:

Nutrition
Health
Behavior
Production


→ Names are the only shared language

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
What Is a Naming Convention?

A set of agreed‑upon rules for creating unique, informative, and stable names for data objects.

Applies to:

Variables
Tables
Files
Visuals
Ontology terms

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Naming ≠ Style Preference
Bad naming causes:

Wrong joins
Broken pipelines
Invalid within/between farm comparisons

These errors do not throw exceptions.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Common Dairy Data Failures

DIM, days_in_milk, DaysInMilk
milk_yield, MilkYieldKg, MY
other, Other, OTHER
lactation_stage vs lactation_stages

Each creates a new semantic entity

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Names Encode Meaning
A good name answers:

What?
For whom?
In what unit?
At what level?
Over what time window?

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Core Design Principles

Consistency over cleverness
Explicit over implicit
Stable over convenient
Readable by humans and machines

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Scope of a Naming Convention
You must define rules for:

✅ Variables
✅ Tables
✅ Files
✅ Features
✅ Categories
✅ Ontology terms

Partial conventions always fail.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Case Sensitivity Matters
Choose one and enforce it.
Recommended: - CamelCase for data schemas - lower_snake_case for code variables
Never mix within the same layer.

title: Reproducible and FAIR data science
text:
Variable Naming Pattern
It’s now the perfect time to decide on a convention used for the project

title: Regular expressions
text:
Prerequisites

This chapter will focus on functions that use regular expressions, a concise and powerful language for describing patterns within strings.


# Install necessary packages if not already installed
if(!require(tidyverse)) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!require(babynames)) install.packages("babynames")

# Load the libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(babynames)

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Pattern basics

Letters and numbers match exactly and are called literal characters.
Most punctuation characters, like ., +, *, [,], and ?, have special meanings and are called metacharacters.

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Quantifiers
Quantifiers control how many times a pattern can match:

? makes a pattern optional (i.e. it matches 0 or 1 times)
+ lets a pattern repeat (i.e. it matches at least once)
* lets a pattern be optional or repeat (i.e. it matches any number of times, including 0).

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Character classes
Character classes are defined by [] and let you match a set of characters

[abcd] matches “a”, “b”, “c”, or “d”.
You can also invert the match by starting with ^: [^abcd] matches anything except “a”, “b”, “c”, or “d”.

title: Regular expressions
text:
Example 1
We can use this idea to find the words containing an “x” surrounded by vowels:

str_view(words, "[aeiou]x[aeiou]")

[284] │ <exa>ct
[285] │ <exa>mple
[288] │ <exe>rcise
[289] │ <exi>st

title: Regular expressions
text:
Example 2
We can use this idea to find “y” surrounded by consonants:

str_view(words, "[^aeiou]y[^aeiou]")

[836] │ <sys>tem
[901] │ <typ>e

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Detect matches
str_detect() returns a logical vector that is TRUE if the pattern matches an element of the character vector and FALSE otherwise

str_detect(c("a", "b", "c"), "[aeiou]")

[1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE

title: Regular expressions
text:
Count matches
The next step up in complexity from str_detect() is str_count(): rather than a true or false, it tells you how many matches there are in each string.

x <- c("apple", "banana", "pear")
str_count(x, "p")

[1] 2 0 1

title: Regular expressions
text:
Replace values
We can modify values with str_replace() and str_replace_all().

 str_replace() replaces the first match,
 str_replace_all() replaces all matches.

str_remove() and str_remove_all() are handy shortcuts for str_replace(x, pattern, "")

title: Regular expressions
text:
Replace values

x <- c("apple", "pear", "banana")
str_replace_all(x, "[aeiou]", "-")

[1] "-ppl-"  "p--r"   "b-n-n-"

title: Regular expressions
text:
Extract variables
Consider the following dataset

df <- tribble(
  ~str,
  "<Sheryl>-F_34",
  "<Kisha>-F_45", 
  "<Brandon>-N_33",
  "<Sharon>-F_38", 
  "<Penny>-F_58",
  "<Justin>-M_41", 
  "<Patricia>-F_84", 
)

title: Regular expressions
text:
Extract variables

df |> 
  separate_wider_regex(
    str,
    patterns = c(
      "<", 
      name = "[A-Za-z]+", 
      ">-", 
      gender = ".",
      "_",
      age = "[0-9]+"
    )
  )

# A tibble: 7 × 3
  name     gender age  
  <chr>    <chr>  <chr>
1 Sheryl   F      34   
2 Kisha    F      45   
3 Brandon  N      33   
4 Sharon   F      38   
5 Penny    F      58   
6 Justin   M      41   
7 Patricia F      84

title: Regular expressions
text:
Escaping
In order to match a literal ., you need an escape which tells the regular expression to match metacharacters literally.
Like strings, regexps use the backslash for escaping.

So, to match a ., you need the regexp \..
Unfortunately this creates a problem. We use strings to represent regular expressions, and \ is also used as an escape symbol in strings.

title: Regular expressions
text:
Escaping
So to create the regular expression \. we need the string "\\.", as the following example shows.

# To create the regular expression \., we need to use \\.
dot <- "\\."

# But the expression itself only contains one \
str_view(dot)

[1] │ \.

cat("\n") 
# And this tells R to look for an explicit .
str_view(c("abc", "a.c", "bef"), "a\\.c")

[2] │ <a.c>

title: Regular expressions
text:
Escaping and literal
If \ is used as an escape character in regular expressions, how do you match a literal \?

Well, you need to escape it, creating the regular expression \\. To create that regular expression, you need to use a string, which also needs to escape \. That means to match a literal \ you need to write "\\\\" — you need four backslashes to match one!

title: Regular expressions
text:
Escaping and literal

x <- "a\\b"
str_view(x)

[1] │ a\b

cat("\n") 
str_view(x, "\\\\")

[1] │ a<\>b

title: Regular expressions
text:
Matching literals
If you’re trying to match a literal ., $, |, *, +, ?, {, }, (, ), there’s an alternative to using a backslash escape: you can use a character class: [.], [$], [|], … all match the literal values.

str_view(c("abc", "a.c", "a*c", "a c"), "a[.]c")

[2] │ <a.c>

title: Regular expressions
text:
Anchors
By default, regular expressions will match any part of a string. If you want to match at the start or end you need to anchor the regular expression using ^ to match the start or $ to match the end:

str_view(fruit, "^a")

[1] │ <a>pple
[2] │ <a>pricot
[3] │ <a>vocado

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Anchors
You can also match the boundary between words (i.e. the start or end of a word) with \b.

x <- c("summary(x)", "summarize(df)", "rowsum(x)", "sum(x)")
str_view(x, "sum")

[1] │ <sum>mary(x)
[2] │ <sum>marize(df)
[3] │ row<sum>(x)
[4] │ <sum>(x)

cat("\n") 
str_view(x, "\\bsum\\b")

[4] │ <sum>(x)

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Anchors

str_replace_all("abc", c("$", "^", "\\b"), "--")

[1] "abc--"   "--abc"   "--abc--"

title: Regular expressions
text:
Character classes
A character class, or character set, allows you to match any character in a set. As we discussed above, you can construct your own sets with [], where [abc] matches “a”, “b”, or “c” and [^abc] matches any character except “a”, “b”, or “c”. Apart from ^ there are two other characters that have special meaning inside of []:

- defines a range, e.g., [a-z] matches any lower case letter and [0-9] matches any number.
\ escapes special characters, so [\^\-\]] matches ^, -, or ].

title: Regular expressions
text:
Character classes example 1

x <- "abcd ABCD 12345 -!@#%."
str_view(x, "[abc]+")

[1] │ <abc>d ABCD 12345 -!@#%.

cat("\n") 
str_view(x, "[a-z]+")

[1] │ <abcd> ABCD 12345 -!@#%.

cat("\n") 
str_view(x, "[^a-z0-9]+")

[1] │ abcd< ABCD >12345< -!@#%.>

title: Regular expressions
text:
Character classes example 2

# You need an escape to match characters that are otherwise
# special inside of []
str_view("a-b-c", "[a-c]")

[1] │ <a>-<b>-<c>

cat("\n")
str_view("a-b-c", "[a\\-c]")

[1] │ <a><->b<-><c>

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Character classes
The following code demonstrates the six shortcuts with a selection of letters, numbers, and punctuation characters.

x <- "abcd ABCD 12345 -!@#%."
str_view(x, "\\d+")

[1] │ abcd ABCD <12345> -!@#%.

str_view(x, "\\D+")

[1] │ <abcd ABCD >12345< -!@#%.>

str_view(x, "\\s+")

[1] │ abcd< >ABCD< >12345< >-!@#%.

str_view(x, "\\S+")

[1] │ <abcd> <ABCD> <12345> <-!@#%.>

str_view(x, "\\w+")

[1] │ <abcd> <ABCD> <12345> -!@#%.

str_view(x, "\\W+")

[1] │ abcd< >ABCD< >12345< -!@#%.>

title: Regular expressions
text:
Quantifiers
Quantifiers control how many times a pattern matches. Previously, we learned about ? (0 or 1 matches), + (1 or more matches), and * (0 or more matches). For example, colou?r will match American or British spelling, \d+ will match one or more digits, and \s? will optionally match a single item of whitespace. You can also specify the number of matches precisely with {}:

{n} matches exactly n times.
{n,} matches at least n times.
{n,m} matches between n and m times.

title: Regular expressions
text:
Operator precedence and parentheses

Regular expressions have their own precedence rules
Quantifiers have high precedence
Alternation has low precedence
which means that ab+ is equivalent to a(b+), and ^a|b$ is equivalent to (^a)|(b$).

Just like with algebra, you can use parentheses to override the usual order. But unlike algebra you’re unlikely to remember the precedence rules for regexes, so feel free to use parentheses liberally.

title: Regular expressions
text:
Grouping and capturing
As well as overriding operator precedence, parentheses have another important effect: they create capturing groups that allow you to use sub-components of the match.
For example, the following pattern finds all fruits that have a repeated pair of letters:

str_view(fruit, "(..)\\1")

 [4] │ b<anan>a
[20] │ <coco>nut
[22] │ <cucu>mber
[41] │ <juju>be
[56] │ <papa>ya
[73] │ s<alal> berry

title: Regular expressions
Regular expressions
text:
Grouping and capturing
You can also use back references in str_replace(). For example, this code switches the order of the second and third words in sentences:

sentences |> 
  str_replace("(\\w+) (\\w+) (\\w+)", "\\1 \\3 \\2") |> 
  str_view()

Today’s promise
By the end of this lecture you will be able to:

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done

Agenda

Why we get stuck

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
The real problem isn’t effort

Most students are not lazy — they’re overloaded with inputs:

team messages, emails, Canvas updates, data files, meeting notes
half-formed ideas, “we should…” tasks, unclear responsibilities

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
Typical failure modes

Open loops: you keep thinking “don’t forget…”
Vague tasks: “work on the project” has no action
Hidden work: waiting on someone, but nobody tracks it
Communication mismatch: same message lands differently across people

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
The 5-step workflow
A widely used productivity approach describes five steps:

Capture — collect what has your attention
Clarify — decide what it means
Organize — put it where it belongs
Reflect — review frequently
Engage — do the work with confidence

https://gettingthingsdone.com

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: Rule: one inbox per input type
text:
Rule: one inbox per input type

A single place for:

quick notes

“someone asked me to…”
ideas

What happens when multiple communication channels are used…

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:

Anything you might need to remember later

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
Clariry
For each item in your inbox, decide:

Is it actionable?


No → trash / reference / someday
Yes → what’s the next action?


If it takes <2 minutes, do it now (optional habit)

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done

Next action test
A “next action” must be:

physical/visible (send, call, draft, run, compute)

text:

✅ “Compute SR/CR/PR for last 6 cycles and draft 3 bullet insights”

“Fix the code”

✅ “Re-run notebook, log failing cell, create minimal reproducible example”

The project definition
A project is an outcome that needs multiple actions.

“Finish our dashboard” is not a project.
“Publish a dashboard that computes SR/CR/PR with QA checks and a one-page interpretation” is.

Outcome (one sentence):

Owner:

Next action (must exist):

text:

Columns (recommended for student teams):

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: Why DISC matters for “getting things done”
text:
Why DISC matters for “getting things done”

A task system fails if communication fails.
People differ in:

preferred speed
detail tolerance
comfort with conflict
need for structure

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: If your team mates lean D (Red)
text:
If your team mates lean D (Red)

Lead with the ask + deadline
Offer 2 options, request a decision

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: If your team mates lean I (Yellow)
text:
If your team mates lean I (Yellow)

Start with purpose and energy
Make the ask collaborative (“Let’s decide together”)

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: If your team mates lean S (Green)
text:
If your team mates lean S (Green)

Provide stability: what changes, what doesn’t
Ask for concerns and confirm agreement

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
section: If your team mates lean C (Blue)
text:
If your team mates lean C (Blue)

Provide detail, evidence, definitions
Put assumptions and acceptance criteria in writing

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
Delegation that sticks (DISC + GTD)
When you delegate, don’t just say “can you handle X?”
Use:

Outcome
Definition of Done
Next check-in
Track it on
Waiting For

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
One-sentence delegation format
“Could you deliver [outcome] by [date], with [DoD bullets]? Let’s check in [time].”

title: Lecture — How to Get Things Done
text:
Why weekly reviews matter
Even the best system decays unless you refresh it.
A weekly review is a dedicated time to:

clear inboxes
update next actions
ensure each project has a next action
look ahead at the calendar

The weekly review is widely described as a core habit in GTD-style approaches.
