title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Learning goals

Distinguish cross-sectional vs cohort reproductive analytics
Build a KPI “stack” (events → rates → decisions)
Understand how program design (heat vs TAI) changes denominators
Prepare to deep-dive the metric list (Parts 3–4)

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
The KPI that “runs the farm”
On large dairies, the common high-level metric is the 21-day pregnancy rate (often used as the primary performance metric).
It is determined by:

AI service rate (submission of eligible cows)
Pregnancies per AI (P/AI) at a specified time post-AI

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
A practical decomposition
For a time window (often 21 days):

Insemination rate / risk (IR): among eligible, fraction inseminated
Conception rate / risk (CR): among inseminated, fraction that conceived
Pregnancy rate / risk (PR): among eligible, fraction that became pregnant

Rule of thumb (when windows align): > PR ≈ IR × CR
This is why separating “submission” from “success” matters.

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Cross-sectional analysis - snapshot

“Where are we today?”
Uses animals currently in the herd, current status counts
Examples:

Days open
Days in milk?
Animals inseminated

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Cohort analysis - follow-through

“How did this group perform over time?”
Tracks a defined entry group (e.g., calving week/month)
Examples:

Days to first service for cows calving in January
Pregnancy by 150 DIM for calving cohort
Heifer age at pregnancy for birth cohort

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Why it matters: denominator drift
Cross-sectional denominators:

Can be distorted by culling, moves, missing preg checks, delayed recording

Cohort denominators:

Require good follow-up (loss to follow-up is explicit)
Better for program evaluation (“did Presynch-Ovsynch change outcomes?”)

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
When to use which (rule set)
Use cross-sectional when:

You want rapid monitoring and operational lists
You need current “actionable” counts (who is open, who is overdue)

Use cohort when:

You want causal-ish evaluation of policy changes
You compare periods, protocols, pens, technicians

Best practice: run both—cross-sectional for operations, cohort for learning.

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Metric taxonomy

Timing metrics (intervals)

e.g., DIM to first service, days open, age at first calving

Count/intensity metrics

e.g., inseminations per pregnancy, >3 services

Rate/risk metrics

service rate, conception rate, pregnancy rate, non-return rate

Inventory metrics

pregnant heifers, # eligible not served, total pregnancies

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Monitoring questions
When pregnancy rate is down:

Is it data? (missing heats, late preg checks)
Did eligibility change? (health holds, VWP, pen moves)
Did submission/service change? (IR/service rate)

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Monitoring questions

Did P/AI change? (CR)
Did loss change? (preg loss, return-to-estrus patterns)

This mirrors the management questions emphasized in reproductive programs: how cows are submitted for first AI and how nonpregnant cows are identified and reinseminated.

title: Lecture – Cross-sectional vs cohort analysis analysis
text:
Bias, momentum, variance, lag (definitions)
For every metric, we will reflect on:

Bias: systematic distortion (selection, missingness, censoring, misclassification)
Momentum: inertia due to biology & pipeline (today’s changes reflect past actions)
Variance: random noise (small n, clustering by tech/pen/week)
Lag: delay between management change and metric response

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Learning goals

Define each youngstock reproductive KPI precisely
Add common synonyms/aliases
For each metric: reflect on bias, momentum, variance, lag
What question can each metric answer, or not
Identify minimum data fields needed to compute reliably

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Youngstock KPIs: why they’re different

Heifers are not “DIM-based”; they’re age-based and growth/management dependent
Denominators are often small and strongly affected by:

enrollment criteria (eligible/not eligible)
missing heat detection and unrecorded services
pregnancy confirmation practices

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Data prerequisites (heifers)
Minimum fields:

Birth date, ID, breed
First heat date (if observed/sensor)
First service date(s)
Pregnancy diagnosis date & outcome
Calving date (first calving) or removal date

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Data prerequisites (heifers)
Optional but bias-reducing:

Body weight / ADG, BCS (if used), health events
Pen/group, breeding method (AI vs bull), semen ID

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Age at first heat

Synonyms: age at puberty, age at first estrus
Definition: age (days or months) at first recorded estrus event.
Bias - Under-detection (no sensors, low observation intensity) - “First recorded” ≠ “first occurred”
Momentum - Reflects months of rearing decisions (nutrition, health) → slow to change
Variance - Small cohorts + seasonality in observation/sensors
Lag - Management change today shows up only when new calves reach puberty age

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Age at first insemination

Synonyms: age at first service, age at first AI
Definition: age at first recorded insemination (AI or bull service if recorded).
Bias - Eligibility rules (min BW/age) differ by farm - Unrecorded natural services inflate “late” ages
Momentum - Strongly driven by growth program (pipeline)
Variance - Clustered by breeding season / staffing
Lag - Nutrition changes → growth → later first service effects

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Age at pregnancy

Synonyms: age at conception (often approximated), age at confirmed pregnancy
Definition (recommended): - age at pregnancy confirmation date - optionally infer conception date = AI date + 0 (for AI-based conception)
Bias - Delayed pregnancy diagnosis shifts ages later - Pregnancy loss misclassified if not rechecked
Momentum - Influenced by both service timing and conception success
Variance - Small n; strong clustering by bull/tech
Lag - Program changes impact after heifers reach breeding eligibility

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Expected calving age

Synonyms: predicted calving age, due-date age
Definition: age at expected calving date = pregnancy date + gestation length (standardized or breed-specific).
Bias - If pregnancy date is “confirmation date,” due date is biased late unless corrected - Missing or incorrect gestation length assumptions
Momentum - Mirrors age at pregnancy with additional fixed lag
Variance - Minimal beyond pregnancy date noise
Lag - Inherent gestation lag

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Number of inseminations per heifer

Synonyms: services per heifer, service intensity
Definition: total services recorded for heifers in a defined cohort/time window.
Bias - Missing service records (especially natural service) - Heifers sold/culled mid-process (censoring)
Momentum - Will improve slowly; reflects both estrus detection and fertility
Variance - High at small n; outliers dominate (repeat breeders)
Lag - Responds after enough heifers complete breeding cycle

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Number of heifers pregnant

Synonyms: pregnant heifer inventory
Definition: count of heifers with confirmed pregnancy (cross-sectional).
Bias - Depends on pregnancy check schedule and recording - Pregnancy loss not updated → overcount
Momentum - Inventory responds slowly (pipeline)
Variance - Lower if herd is large; higher for small operations
Lag - Built-in due to breeding + preg-check delay

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
section: Number of youngstock not served > 16 months
text:
Number of youngstock not served > 16 months

Synonyms: overdue heifers, eligible-not-bred
Definition: count of heifers older than threshold (e.g., 16 mo) with no recorded service.
Bias - Missing service records; unrecorded bull exposure - “Eligibility” should ideally include weight/health, not just age
Momentum - Sensitive to backlog (can change fast if you “catch up”)
Variance - Strongly seasonal if breeding is seasonal
Lag - Low (operational metric)

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Age at first calving

Synonyms: AFC (age at first calving)
Definition: age at first calving date.
Bias - Sold/cull heifers excluded (survivorship bias) - Induced calving / abortion not handled consistently
Momentum - Highly “sticky”: reflects months/years of upstream decisions
Variance - Moderate; depends on cohort size
Lag - Long (breeding + gestation)

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Conception rate first insemination (heifers)

Synonyms: first-service conception rate, P/AI at 1st service
Definition: among heifers receiving first service, proportion confirmed pregnant from that service.
Bias - Pregnancy diagnosis timing and loss confound “conception” - If rechecks aren’t done, early loss misclassified as failure or success
Momentum - Can change faster than AFC but still requires preg-check pipeline
Variance - High when few first services occur
Lag - At least until pregnancy confirmation

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Total number of pregnancies (heifers)

Synonyms: total heifer pregnancies, preg count
Definition: total confirmed pregnancies in a time window (cross-sectional or cohort).
Bias - Double counting if rechecks create duplicate pregnancy events - Under-count if no confirmation recorded
Momentum - Inventory-like
Variance - Depends on heifer flow
Lag - Driven by pregnancy diagnosis schedule

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Total number of inseminations (heifers)

Definition: total recorded services for heifers over a period.
Bias - Missing recording, bull exposure, duplicate entries
Momentum - Responds quickly to management changes (technique, scheduling)
Variance - High if only a few breeding days
Lag - Minimal (event metric)

title: Lecture - Youngstock metrics
text:
Implementation notes (heifer dashboard)
Recommended visuals:

Cohort waterfall: birth → first service → pregnant → calved
Histogram: age at first service, age at pregnancy, AFC
“Overdue” list: >16 mo not served (action list)

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Why Inventory Calculations Matter

Inventory is the constraint layer under everything else.
If a herd inventory snapshot is wrong → future decisions will all be wrong.
Today:

Build the mental model
Understand the math
Lab on CDREC flow

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Learning Outcomes
By the end you can:

Distinguish state vs flow
Define risk vs rate
Compute and interpret key herd dynamics metrics
Avoid synonym traps
Use DC305 to extract numerators/denominators for CDREC strategy

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
section: Two herds, same cow count, different future
text:
Two herds, same cow count, different future

Herd A and Herd B both have 1,000 cows today
One shrinks, one grows
The difference is flows






flowchart LR
Births --> Heifers --> Cows
Cows -->|Cull/Sell| Exit
Cows -->|Death| Exit

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Vocabulary

Statics: snapshot at a moment

“what exists”

Dynamics: movement over time

“where it is going”

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
DC305
Choose the “as‑of” date intentionally
Set date in cowfile to “today”
SETDAY\*
Set date to last event entry date
SETDAY\E
Set date to most recent test date
SETDAY\T
Set date to selected date
SETDAY\mm/dd/yy

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Why date matters

“Last 365 days” depends on file date
If the backup date is wrong → your rates are wrong

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Risk vs Rate (the core confusion)
Most bad inventory math comes from mixing:

Probabilities and
Time‑based rates

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Risk (probability)

Fraction experiencing event over specified period
Unitless
Must specify time window

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Example - Culling Risk
Definition
Probability that a cow is culled during a specified time period.
\[
Risk = \frac{\text{Number of cows culled during period}}{\text{Number of cows at risk at start}}
\]

Unit: none (0–1)
Time period must be defined
Bounded by 1

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Better example - Annual Culling Risk
Suppose:

100 cows at start of year
20 cows culled during the year

\[
Risk = 20/100 = 0.20
\]
👉 20% probability of being culled during that year
This is a probability over one year.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Rate

Events per unit time (per cow‑time)
Units: 1/time
Supports unequal follow‑up

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Incidence Rate
Definition
Speed at which culling events occur.
\[
Rate = \frac{\text{Number of cullings}}{\text{Total cow-time at risk}}
\]

Unit: 1 / time (e.g., per cow-year)
Uses animal-time
No upper bound

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Cow-year — definition
A cow-year is animal-time at risk:

1 cow-year = one cow present and at risk for 1 year
More generally: sum the time each cow is “in the population at risk,” expressed in years.

This is the recommended denominator logic for herd turnover: exits / animal time at risk.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Cow-year — formula
\[
\text{Cow-years} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\text{days cow } i \text{ is at risk during period}}{365}
\]
Key idea: time belongs in the denominator when you report a rate.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Example: counting cow-years

Cow A present all year: (365/365 = 1.00) cow-year
Cow B present 180 days: (180/365 = 0.49) cow-year
Cow C present 90 days: (90/365 = 0.25) cow-year

Total exposure = (1.00 + 0.49 + 0.25 = 1.74) cow-years

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Herd-level approximation
For a 12-month window, if herd size is fairly stable:
\[
\text{Cow-years} \approx \text{Average number of cows present during the year} \times \text{1 year}
\]
This is why many “annual” turnover metrics use average milking + dry cows as the denominator.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Example: Annual Culling Rate
Suppose:

20 cows culled
Total time at risk = 90 cow-years
(because cows leave during the year)

\[
Rate = 20 / 90 = 0.222 \text{ per cow-year}
\]
👉 0.222 cullings per cow-year
This is a speed, not a probability.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
section: Why They Are Not the Same
text:
Why They Are Not the Same
Risk asks:

What fraction of cows are culled during the year?

Rate asks:

How fast are cows being culled?

Different denominators:

Risk → cows
Rate → cow-time

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Why words break math

“30% cull risk” - Over a month? year? lactation?
“0.35 removals per cow‑year” - interpretable

Rule

If you can’t attach a time window, you don’t have a rate.

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Definitions that won’t betray you
Each metric has a:

numerator
denominator
time window
population scope

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Removal / Culling rate — definition

Exits / cow‑year
Confusion

Exits may include sold (alive) + culled + deaths (be explicit)
Sometimes used as synonyms
In rigorous use: removal includes all exits; cull may exclude deaths/live culls

Your job: define numerator explicitly

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Mortality / Death rate — definition

Deaths / cow‑year
Always specify population:

cows
calves
heifers

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Replacement rate — definition

First‑lactation entries / cow‑year
In a steady state (no growth) :

replacement rate ≈ exit rate

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Calving / Freshening rate — definition

Calvings / cow‑year
Sometimes operationalized as fresh cows per month (be explicit)

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Herd turnover rate — definition

Proportion replaced over time
Think “stall slots” being refilled

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Involuntary vs voluntary removals

Voluntary culling → economically driven decision

Low milk yield
Reproductive decisions
Strategic replacement

Involuntary culling → biological constraint

Mastitis
Lameness
Mortality

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
Build it from components
Inventory is not one number.
It is:

entries
exits
time

title: Inventory Management in Dairy Systems
text:
The simplest dynamic identity
\[
N_{t+1} = N_t + \text{Entries} - \text{Exits}
\]
Stable size implies:
\[
\text{Entry rate} \approx \text{Exit rate}
\]

What changes herd size?
If you want growth:

increase entries
decrease exits
or both

…but constraints intervene.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Goals
It’s rare that a data analysis involves only a single data frame. Typically you have many data frames, and you must join them together to answer the questions that you’re interested in.

Mutating joins, which add new variables to one data frame from matching observations in another.
Filtering joins, which filter observations from one data frame based on whether or not they match an observation in another (conditional match.

For more information see this link https://r4ds.hadley.nz/joins.html

title: Joining datasets
text: Setup

title: Joining datasets
text:
Primary key

Every join involves a pair of keys: a primary key (or keys) and a foreign key (or keys).
A primary key is a variable or set of variables that uniquely identifies each observation.
When more than one variable is needed, the key is called a compound key.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Foreign key
A foreign key is a variable (or set of variables) that corresponds to a primary key in another table.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Visualizing key relationships
Often it helps to visualize the keys and their relationships.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Best practices

Have the primary and foreign keys have the same names accross the dataframes.
Have the other columns different names (to avoid confusion after joining).
Verify that keys do indeed uniquely identify each observation.
Sometimes you will have to create a key by combining columns generating a surrogate key.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Overview
The joins shown here are the so-called equi joins, where rows match if the keys are equal (inner and outer joins)

title: Joining datasets
text:
Inner join (A)

Rows are retained if and only if the keys are equal.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Inner join (B)
There are three possible outcomes for a row in x:

If it doesn’t match anything, it’s dropped.
If it matches 1 row in y, it’s preserved.
If it matches more than 1 row in y, it’s duplicated once for each match.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Outer joins

Left join
Right join
Full join

title: Joining datasets
text:
Left join

A left join keeps all observations in x
Every row of x is preserved in the output because it can fall back to matching a row of NAs in y.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Right join

A right join keeps all observations in y, 
Every row of y is preserved in the output because it can fall back to matching a row of NAs in x.
The output still matches x as much as possible; any extra rows from y are added to the end.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Full join

A full join keeps all observations that appear in x or y, 
Every row of x and y is included in the output because both x and y have a fall back row of NAs.
Again, the output starts with all rows from x, followed by the remaining unmatched y rows.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Semi join

The semi-join keeps rows in x that have one or more matches in y

title: Joining datasets
text:
Anti join

The anti-join keeps rows in x that match zero rows in y,

title: Joining datasets
text:
Introduction to non-equi
Basic idea of non-equi joins

title: Joining datasets
text:
Types of non-equi joins

Cross joins match every pair of rows.
Inequality joins use <, <=, >, and >= instead of ==.
Rolling joins are similar to inequality joins but only find the closest match.
Overlap joins are a special type of inequality join designed to work with ranges.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Cross join

A cross join matches everything, generating the Cartesian product of rows.
This means the output will have nrow(x) * nrow(y) rows.
Sometimes called a self-join.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Inequality joins

Inequality joins use <, <=, >=, or > to restrict the set of possible matches

title: Joining datasets
text:
Rolling joins

Rolling joins are a special type of inequality join where instead of getting every row that satisfies the inequality, you get just the closest row.

title: Joining datasets
text:
Overlap joins

between(x, y_lower, y_upper) is short for x >= y_lower, x <= y_upper.
within(x_lower, x_upper, y_lower, y_upper) is short for x_lower >= y_lower, x_upper <= y_upper.
overlaps(x_lower, x_upper, y_lower, y_upper) is short for x_lower <= y_upper, x_upper >= y_lower.

title: Lecture 6 - Milk production data (II)
text:
Calculation of Breeding Values
\[
y = Xb + Zu + e
\]

y = observed phenotypes

b = fixed effects (e.g., sex, management)

u = random genetic effects (breeding values)

e = residual error

X, Z = link observations to effects

title: Lecture 6 - Milk production data (II)
section: What do we get from this?
text:
What do we get from this?

Each animal receives an estimate of genetic merit

Called Predicted Transmitting Ability (PTA)

PTA = breeding value ÷ 2

Note:
Real U.S. animal models are more complex and include: - Contemporary groups
- Herd-by-sire effects
- Permanent environmental effects

title: Lecture 6 - Milk production data (II)
text:
Genomic prediction
What is genomic prediction? - Uses DNA information + phenotypes + pedigree to estimate genetic merit - DNA markers = SNP (positions in the genome with two variants)
Why use genomics? - DNA is not influenced by environment - Improvs accuracy of breeding value estimation - Allows evaluation of young animals without records

title: Lecture 6 - Milk production data (II)
text:
How are genomic PTA calculated?

Estimate SNP effects using a predictor population with reliable PTA
Combine:

Genomic predictions from SNP
Parent averages (PA)
Traditional pedigree + phenotype evaluations

title: Lecture 6 - Milk production data (II)
text:
Net merit dollar value
The expected difference in lifetime profit (in USD) of a cow (or bull’s daughters) compared to a reference animal, due solely to genetics, assuming typical U.S. management and prices.
“If I replace one genetically average cow with this one, how much more (or less) money will I make over her lifetime?”

title: Week Overview: Herd Inventory Calculations (Lecture 50 min + Lab)
text:
Inventory calculations are the bridge between biology and decisions. If your snapshot is wrong, your reproduction strategy, semen strategy (sexed vs beef), housing plan, and growth plan will all be wrong—even if your math is perfect.
This week trains students to: 1) produce a defensible snapshot of the current herd and 2) compute flows (dynamics) with correct denominators and time windows, then 3) translate those numbers into a growth strategy for CDREC (CURC).

Reproductive metrics: conception rate, days to first service, pregnancy rate
Heat detection data and estrus synchronization records
Introduction to event-based data analysis



Activities/Tasks

Lecture (2/19): Reproductive biology basics and key data-driven metrics
Lab (2/19):

CURC Visit #2: Observe reproductive management practices (AI, pregnancy checks)
Collect/discuss available reproductive data from CURC records
Brainstorm questions: How can reproductive data improve management?




Due Dates:

Reflection assignment on CURC visit (due start of Week 6)



Datasets/Resources:

CURC reproductive records (breeding dates, pregnancy outcomes)

title: WEEK 10: Nutrition & Feed Management Data
text:
Details/Subtopics:

Total mixed ration (TMR) composition and analysis
Feed intake monitoring
Body condition scoring (BCS) and relationships to nutrition/production



Activities/Tasks

Lecture (3/24): Nutrition basics and data-driven feeding strategies
Lecture (3/26): Interpreting feed analysis reports and intake data
Lab (3/26):

CURC Visit #3: Observe feed preparation, delivery, and intake measurement
Review CURC feed records and TMR composition data
Brainstorm: How do nutrition data inform management decisions?




Due Dates:

Reflection on nutrition systems (due after Spring Break)



Datasets/Resources:

CURC feed composition, intake records, BCS data

title: Course support
text: Most of you will need help at some point and we want to make sure you can identify when that is without getting too frustrated and feel comfortable seeking help.

title: Course support
text:
Lectures and labs
If you have a question during lecture or lab, feel free to ask it! There are likely other students with the same question, so by asking you will create a peer teaching opportunity for everyone.

title: Course support
text:
Office hours
The entire teaching team is here to help you be successful in the course. You are encouraged to ask questions about the course content and assignments. A lot of questions are most effectively answered in-person, so pass by during office hours. I encourage each and every one of you to take advantage of this resource! If you truly have no questions to ask, just stop by and say hi and introduce yourself.
Teaching assistants will be available during these hours if you require help or feedback.

title: Course support
text:
Discussion forum
Have a question that might be valuable for all course participant? Prefer to write out your question in detail rather than asking in person? The online discussion forum is the best venue for these! We will use github issue as the online discussion forum. There is a chance another student has already asked a similar question, so please check the other posts before adding a new question. If you know the answer to a question that is posted, I encourage you to respond!

title: Course support
text:
Email
Please refrain from emailing any course content questions (those should go here github issue), and only use email for questions about personal matters that may not be appropriate for the public course forum (e.g., illness, accommodations, etc.).
For such matters, you may email Dr. Miel Hostens at miel.hostens@cornell.edu.

title: Course support
text:
Course materials costs
There are no costs associated with this course. All readings will come from freely available, open resources (open-source textbooks, journal articles, etc.).

title: Course support
text:
Assistance with Teams
Teams will be used for online office hours as well as as a backup option should we need to hold the course online instead of in person.

title: Lab 0 - Meet + greet
text:
Today’s lab is short and sweet! We just need you to fill out the “Getting to know you” survey. Please go here to take it. You will need to log on to Sakai to access to survey.
Your answers can be brief. Some of your answers will be used to guide what application examples might be of interest to a majority of students in the course and some of your answers will be used to help guide team formation. We expect this will take you ~10 minutes.

text:
